worth_it_explanation: Comscore Campaign Ratings, now branded Cross-Platform Campaign Results, reports deduplicated unique reach, average frequency, gross rating points, and on-target percentage for a campaign across desktop, mobile, and connected TV. These checks confirm that a campaign's delivered reach and frequency are evaluated against planned goals, that budget is not concentrated on repeated exposures to the same people while incremental reach goes unmeasured, and that the cross-screen deduplication CCR provides is actually used rather than treating device-level reach as a proxy for person-level reach. The fixes are process changes and reporting configuration adjustments inside the CCR portal, not campaign rebuilds, and they make the post-campaign story credible.
intro: Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) reports deduplicated reach, frequency, and gross rating points (GRPs) for a digital campaign across desktop, mobile, and connected TV (CTV). Reach counts the distinct people a campaign reached within a measurement period. Frequency counts how many times, on average, each reached person was exposed to the campaign. GRPs combine reach and frequency into a single index: reach expressed as a percentage of the target population multiplied by average frequency. On-target percentage reports what share of delivered impressions reached people within the campaign's defined demographic target. These checks confirm that reach and frequency goals are set before a campaign launches so the delivered numbers have a benchmark to compare against, that frequency is managed so budget extends reach rather than concentrating exposures on the same people, and that CCR's cross-screen deduplication is applied so a person reached on a phone and on a laptop is counted once rather than twice.

audit_checks.title: Reach and frequency goals are absent from the media plan, leaving no benchmark to evaluate CCR delivery
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without planned reach and frequency targets documented before a campaign launches, the delivered numbers from Comscore Campaign Ratings cannot be evaluated as on-track or off-track. The team receives a report showing reach and frequency figures but has no reference point to determine whether the campaign covered the intended audience or whether frequency is in range.

educate.base:
Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) measures what a campaign delivered: deduplicated unique reach across desktop, mobile, and connected TV (CTV), average frequency per person, gross rating points (GRPs), and on-target percentage within a defined demographic. These are delivery metrics. They answer 'what did the campaign produce?' They can only be evaluated as good or poor against a planned goal.

A media plan for an awareness or reach-focused campaign should specify a target reach percentage (the share of the intended demographic the campaign aims to reach within the flight period), an average frequency target or range (the number of times, on average, each reached person should see the campaign), and a GRP target that reflects both. When those targets are absent, the CCR report delivers accurate numbers that have no context. A 45% reach figure may be exactly on plan or substantially short depending on what was planned; without the plan documented, there is no way to tell.

Setting goals before the campaign launches also informs pacing decisions mid-flight. If CCR shows that reach is extending efficiently at a low frequency, the campaign is on track. If reach has plateaued and average frequency is climbing, that signals that the campaign is re-serving the same people and that budget adjustments may be needed before the flight ends. Neither conclusion is reachable without a planned benchmark to compare against.

A few signs this check applies:
  • The campaign brief or media plan does not document a target reach percentage, frequency range, or GRP goal for the CCR measurement period
  • The post-campaign CCR report is the first time reach, frequency, and GRP targets are discussed, meaning the team is reverse-engineering goals from the delivered numbers
  • Different stakeholders cite different success criteria when reviewing the CCR output, suggesting targets were not agreed and documented before launch
You may want to confirm that planned reach, frequency, and GRP targets are written into the media plan and shared with all stakeholders before a campaign that uses CCR measurement begins.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser running a brand awareness campaign ahead of a seasonal period, the CCR GRP target should reflect the audience coverage needed to support the downstream conversion campaign. You may want to confirm that the brand team and the performance team agreed on a reach target before the flight, so the CCR output can be evaluated against a shared benchmark rather than interpreted differently by each team.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running an upper-funnel display or video campaign, the CCR reach percentage target should reflect how large a primed audience the team expects to build before retargeting begins. You may want to confirm that the planned reach target is documented before the flight launches, so mid-flight pacing decisions about budget and frequency can be grounded in whether the campaign is on track to build that audience.

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore CCR portal documentation covering goal-setting and benchmarking against media plan targets is accessible to credentialed account holders through the CCR reporting portal. Contact your Comscore account representative for guidance on configuring planned targets and comparing them against delivered reach, frequency, and GRP in the CCR interface.

impactful_updates.update_title: Comscore Campaign Ratings renamed Cross-Platform Campaign Results (December 2025)
impactful_updates.update_summary: Comscore renamed Campaign Ratings to Cross-Platform Campaign Results (CCR) in December 2025, expanding the suite to include streaming audio and social measurement alongside the existing digital, CTV, and TV measurement. The reach, frequency, GRP, and on-target percentage metrics and the CCR acronym carry forward under the new name.

investigate.intro: Review the media plan or campaign brief to confirm whether reach, frequency, and GRP goals are documented for the CCR measurement period, and whether those goals were set before the campaign launched.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Log in to the Comscore CCR reporting portal and open the campaign in question. Note the flight dates and the target demographic defined for the measurement.

Check the campaign documentation (brief, media plan, or IO) for a target reach percentage, a planned average frequency or frequency range, and a GRP goal aligned with the CCR measurement period. These targets should be present before the campaign launched.

If no planned targets are documented, confirm with the media planner or campaign manager whether goals exist in an offline document. Note whether the targets were shared with all stakeholders before the flight began.

Compare any documented targets against the CCR report's delivered reach, frequency, and GRP for the same period. Note the delta and whether a conclusion about performance is reachable from the comparison.
If the team is setting or adjusting targets after reviewing the CCR delivery numbers, document this as a finding: goals were not established before the campaign launched.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether planned reach, frequency, and GRP targets exist in the media plan and whether they were documented before the campaign launched. A campaign with documented targets set before launch passes. A campaign with no documented targets, or with targets written after the CCR report was reviewed, is a fail, recorded with the campaign name and the fields missing from the pre-launch documentation.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether planned reach, frequency, and GRP targets were documented before this campaign launched to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s media plan did not include documented reach, frequency, or gross rating point (GRP) targets for the Comscore Campaign Ratings measurement period. Without planned targets set before the campaign launched, the delivered numbers cannot be evaluated as on-track or off-track, and mid-flight pacing decisions about budget and frequency have no benchmark to reference. {{voice_recommend}} documenting target reach percentage, average frequency range, and GRP goal in the media plan before each campaign that uses CCR measurement begins, and sharing those targets with all stakeholders so the CCR output can be evaluated against a consistent benchmark.

audit_checks.title: Average frequency is high while incremental reach has plateaued, indicating budget is re-serving the same people
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a campaign's reach curve has flattened and average frequency continues to rise, additional impressions are going to people already reached rather than extending coverage to new people. This is a direct budget-efficiency problem: the same spend that could build incremental reach is instead adding exposures to an audience already saturated, while people in the target demographic who have not yet been reached go untouched.

educate.base:
Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) tracks both cumulative deduplicated reach and average frequency over the life of a campaign. In the early days of a flight, each additional impression day typically adds meaningful new reach because the campaign has not yet saturated the target population. Over time, the incremental reach added per impression dollar decreases as the campaign reaches more of the reachable audience. At some point the reach curve flattens: additional budget adds few or no new people, and average frequency climbs instead.

This inflection point, where incremental reach per dollar drops sharply while average frequency continues to rise, is the signal that the campaign has saturated its current audience. Budget spent past this point is spent re-serving the same people rather than extending coverage. Whether that is the right choice depends on the campaign goal. For an awareness campaign trying to maximize the number of people who recall the brand, continued spending past the saturation point is rarely the best use of budget. For a campaign intentionally building frequency to drive consideration, a rising average frequency may be appropriate if the target frequency level is within the planned range.

CCR's frequency distribution report breaks the reached audience into groups by how many times they were exposed: reached once, twice, three times, and so on. This distribution tells the story more precisely than average frequency alone. A high average frequency driven by a small group of people who were served far more times than the rest of the audience indicates a concentration problem, where a subset of the reachable population is receiving a disproportionate share of impressions while the broader target demographic remains unreached.

A few signs this pattern is present:
  • CCR shows week-over-week reach growth slowing or stopping while average frequency continues to rise across the same period
  • The frequency distribution report shows a long tail of people reached at very high frequency, pulling the average up while the median frequency is lower
  • Frequency capping set on the buying platform does not match the frequency levels CCR is actually reporting, suggesting caps are set too high, are not applied consistently across all placements, or are not functioning as expected
You may want to review the CCR frequency distribution alongside the reach curve before mid-flight budget decisions to identify whether additional spend is likely to extend reach or add frequency to already-reached people.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser running a broad awareness campaign ahead of a promotional period, a plateaued reach curve with rising frequency suggests the campaign has covered the reachable audience within its current targeting parameters. You may want to consider whether broadening the targeting, shifting budget to new placements or inventory sources, or adjusting frequency caps on the buy side would extend reach to people not yet covered before the promotional period begins.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser building an upper-funnel audience before a retargeting phase, a high-frequency, low-incremental-reach pattern means the primed audience being built may be smaller than planned because budget is concentrating on a subset of the target. You may want to confirm the CCR reach curve against the planned primed-audience size before the retargeting phase launches.

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore CCR portal documentation covering the frequency distribution report, reach curve analysis, and incremental reach metrics is accessible to credentialed account holders through the CCR reporting portal. Contact your Comscore account representative for guidance on reading the frequency distribution and reach curve together to identify saturation.

investigate.intro: Review the CCR reach curve and frequency distribution report to determine whether incremental reach has plateaued while average frequency is rising, and compare reported frequency against any frequency caps set on the buying platform.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Log in to the Comscore CCR reporting portal and open the campaign's reach and frequency report. Locate the cumulative reach chart or table that shows deduplicated reach by day or week across the flight.

Note whether the week-over-week or day-over-day incremental reach gain is decreasing over time. A reach curve that was adding, for example, 3 percentage points of target-population reach per week early in the flight but is now adding fewer than 0.5 points per week while the campaign is still spending at the same rate is a sign that reach is plateauing.

Open the frequency distribution report. Note the share of the reached audience exposed once, twice, three times, and at higher frequencies. If a substantial share of the reached audience is at five or more exposures while overall reach is flat, the campaign is concentrating impressions on already-reached people.

Compare the average frequency CCR reports against the frequency cap configured on the buying platform (the demand-side platform (DSP) or ad server used to run the campaign). Note whether the cap appears to be functioning as configured or whether actual average frequency substantially exceeds the cap.

Note whether the campaign uses multiple placements or inventory sources. A campaign running across several DSPs or publishers may have frequency caps set per placement that do not limit total cross-placement frequency, allowing CCR to show a higher total frequency than any individual placement's cap would suggest.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the weekly incremental reach trend from the CCR reach curve, the frequency distribution showing the share of the audience at each exposure level, and whether the frequency cap on the buying platform aligns with reported average frequency. A campaign with healthy incremental reach growth and a frequency distribution concentrated at low exposure counts passes. A campaign with a flat reach curve, a high average frequency, and a frequency distribution showing a significant share of the audience at five or more exposures is a fail, recorded with the average frequency, the incremental reach trend, and the frequency cap setting on the buying platform.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the weekly incremental reach trend, the frequency distribution, and the buying-platform frequency cap setting above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Comscore Campaign Ratings report shows a plateaued reach curve alongside rising average frequency, indicating that additional impressions are being delivered to people already reached rather than extending coverage to new people in the target demographic. The frequency distribution report shows a concentration of exposures at high frequency levels, pulling average frequency up while incremental reach remains flat. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the CCR reach curve and frequency distribution at a regular cadence during each flight, confirming that frequency caps set on the buying platform are consistent with the CCR-reported average frequency, and considering targeting adjustments or new inventory sources to extend reach when the curve flattens before the planned flight end.

audit_checks.title: Reach is reported at the device level rather than using CCR cross-screen deduplication, overstating the number of distinct people reached
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a person is counted separately on their phone, their laptop, and their connected TV, the reported reach is inflated by the number of devices rather than the number of people. A campaign that appears to have reached 8 million unique browsers and devices may have reached a much smaller number of distinct people, making the on-target percentage and frequency calculations unreliable for decision-making.

educate.base:
Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) is built around person-level, cross-screen deduplicated reach: a single person who sees an ad on a phone, a laptop, and a connected TV (CTV) counts as one reached person in the CCR report, not three. This deduplication is one of the primary reasons to use CCR rather than reading reach directly from ad server logs or buying platform reports, which count at the browser or device level and cannot link exposures across devices to the same individual.

Reaching the person-level deduplicated number requires that CCR measurement tags are deployed on all placements and inventory sources included in the campaign, including CTV placements. If a campaign runs across display, mobile web, and CTV but CCR tags are only applied to the display and mobile placements, the CTV impressions are not incorporated into the cross-screen deduplication. The reach number CCR reports in that case reflects only part of the campaign, and the total reported reach is understated. Conversely, if the team reads reach from the buying platform or ad server instead of from CCR, they are reading device-level or browser-level reach, which double-counts people reached on multiple devices and overstates unique person reach.

CCR's cross-screen deduplication relies on Comscore's identity graph, which links device-level signals to person-level audience segments using a combination of deterministic and probabilistic methods. The output is a modeled estimate, not a deterministic count, but it produces a reach figure that more accurately represents the number of distinct people in the target demographic who were exposed to the campaign than any device-level count does.

A few signs that cross-screen deduplication may not be applied correctly:
  • The campaign's reported reach comes from the buying platform or ad server rather than from the CCR portal, meaning the number reflects device-level or browser-level unique reach rather than person-level deduplicated reach
  • CCR tags are applied to display and mobile placements but not to CTV placements, so the CTV portion of the campaign is excluded from the person-level deduplication
  • The CCR report shows a lower total reach than the sum of the individual platform reach numbers would suggest, and the team treats the higher platform-level numbers as more accurate rather than understanding that cross-screen deduplication removes the double-counting that produces the higher figure
You may want to confirm that CCR tags are deployed across all inventory sources in the campaign before the flight begins, so the cross-screen deduplication applies to the full campaign rather than a subset of placements.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser running a campaign across display, mobile, and CTV, the difference between device-level reach and person-level deduplicated reach can be significant if CTV is a large share of the budget. You may want to confirm that CCR tags are on the CTV placements so the person who saw the campaign on their television and on their phone is counted once in the reach total.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser evaluating how efficiently the campaign is building an audience for retargeting, a device-level reach number overstates the primed audience size and leads to an inflated expectation for the retargeting pool. You may want to confirm that the reach number used for retargeting audience planning comes from the CCR person-level deduplicated report rather than from ad server device counts.

educate.learn_more_note: Comscore CCR portal documentation covering tag deployment requirements for cross-screen deduplication, including CTV tag implementation, is accessible to credentialed account holders through the CCR reporting portal. Contact your Comscore account representative for tag deployment guides and confirmation that your CTV placements are incorporated into the person-level deduplication.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether CCR tags are deployed across all placements in the campaign, including CTV, and whether the team is reading reach from the CCR person-level deduplicated report rather than from buying-platform or ad server device-level reports.

comscore.label: Comscore
steps: Log in to the Comscore CCR reporting portal and open the campaign. Review the placement list or tag deployment confirmation to identify which placements and inventory sources have CCR tags active.

Check whether CTV placements are listed alongside display and mobile placements in the CCR tag deployment. If CTV is absent, the cross-screen deduplication does not include television exposure, and the reach figure is person-level only for the non-CTV portion of the campaign.

Open the CCR campaign reach report and note the total deduplicated unique reach figure. Compare this against the total unique reach or unique device count reported by the buying platform or ad server for the same date range.

If the buying platform or ad server number is substantially higher than the CCR deduplicated number, confirm with the team which number is being used for reporting and audience planning. The higher device-level or browser-level number includes double-counting of people reached across devices; the CCR number removes that duplication.

Ask the team whether any portion of the campaign's CTV or streaming inventory was purchased through a channel that does not support CCR tags (e.g., certain programmatic CTV inventory sources or direct publisher buys without a tag integration). Note those placements as excluded from the cross-screen deduplication.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records which placements have CCR tags active, whether CTV is included, and whether the team is using the CCR person-level deduplicated reach number or a device-level count for reporting and planning. A campaign with CCR tags on all major placements including CTV, and with reach reported from the CCR portal rather than from ad server or buying platform device counts, passes. A campaign missing CCR tags on CTV or relying on device-level reach figures for reporting is a fail, recorded with the placements missing tags and the source of the reach number currently used.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which placements have CCR tags active, whether CTV is included, and whether the reach figure in use is person-level deduplicated or device-level above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that reach reporting for {{site_name}}'s campaign is drawn from buying platform or ad server device-level counts rather than from the Comscore Campaign Ratings (CCR) person-level deduplicated report, or that CCR tags are not deployed across all placements including connected TV (CTV), excluding part of the campaign from cross-screen deduplication. Device-level counts include a separate entry for each browser and device a person uses, so a person reached on a phone, a laptop, and a television appears as three reached devices rather than one reached person. {{voice_recommend}} confirming that CCR tags are deployed on all placements in the campaign including CTV before each flight, and using the CCR deduplicated unique reach figure rather than ad server device counts for all reporting and audience planning.
